content.full_text:
82002

EDMUND G. BROWN JR., GOVERNOR

CALIFORNIA
ETHICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1600 K Street
Suite 95814
322-5660

December 9, 1975

Mr. Robert C. Summers
Chief, Political Reform Audit Division
Franchise Tax Board
P.O. Box 1468
Sacramento Ca. 95807

Dear Mr. Summers:

This letter responds to your memorandum of November 20,
addressed to Robert Stern. From the facts stated therein,
I cannot give you a specific answer to the questions posed
therein. However, this letter sets out some considerations
which are relevant in making that determination.

The definition of "lobbyist" as set forth in Government
Code Section 82039 includes any person who is employed or
contracts for economic consideration for the purpose of in-
fluencing administrative action. "Administrative action"
is defined in Section 82002 to include "any rate-making
proceeding," and therefore includes the PUC rate case posited
in your memorandum. The activities of the utility company
employee may constitute "influencing ... administrative action"
as that term is defined in Section 82032. However, from the
facts you have stated it appears that this particular employee
spends most of his time in background research and activities
in support of those who actually represent the utility company
in the rate making proceeding. In regulation, the Commission
has determined that "time spent on research (the gathering of
information, statistics, studies, analyses and the preparation
of legal pleadings, briefs, memoranda)" is not includable for
purposes of determining whether the employee spends a "sub-
stantial or regular" portion of his time in lobbying activities.
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239(e)(4). Therefore, the background
studies and research are not considered to be lobbying activities.

On the other hand, it would appear that some of this employee's
activities constitute "influencing administrative action" because
he works with auditors of the PUC who are investigating the
application ..." and "also has direct communication with other
members of the PUC staff." Considering only the activities which
are not under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239(e)(4), you must
determine whether the employee meets any of the time tests set
forth in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239, paragraphs (e)(2)(A)
through (D). Under the facts you have stated, this employee
might meet the requirements of paragraph (e)(2)(A), that is
spending forty hours in two consecutive months influencing admin-
istrative activity, of which ten hours are spent in direct communi-
cation.

7

Mr. Robert C. Summers
December 9, 1975
Page Two

The above discussion has been limited to administrative
action, because your letter did not indicate any involvement in
legislative action. In addition, you should be aware that the
utility company, if it employs a lobbyist, must report the time
spent by this employee in developing the material on which the
lobbyist's communications are based, even though these activities are not includable by the employee in determining whether
he is a lobbyist. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18620. The regulation does not apply if the employee spends less than ten percent of his time in developing such material. Copies of 2 Cal.
Adm. Code 18239 and 18620 are enclosed.

I hope this information will be of assistance.

Sincerely,

Natalie E. West
Staff Counsel

Enclosures

From the facts stated therein, I cannot give you a specific answer to the questions posed therein. However, this letter sets out some considerations which are relevant in making that determination. The definition of "lobbyist" as set forth in Government Code Section 82039 includes any person who is employed or contracts for economic consideration for the purpose of influencing administrative action. "Administrative action" is defined in Section 82002 to include "any rate-making proceeding," and therefore includes the PUC rate case posited in your memorandum. The activities of the utility company employee may constitute "influencing ... administrative action" as that term is defined in Section 82032. However, from the facts you have stated it appears that this particular employee spends most of his time in background research and activities in support of those who actually represent the utility company in the rate making proceeding. In regulation, the Commission has determined that "time spent on research (the gathering of information, statistics, studies, analyses and the preparation of legal pleadings, briefs, memoranda)" is not includable for purposes of determining whether the employee spends a "substantial or regular" portion of his time in lobbying activities. 2 Cal. On the other hand, it would appear that some of this employee's activities constitute "influencing administrative action" because "he works with auditors of the PUC who are investigating the application ..." and "also has direct communication with other members of the PUC staff." Considering only the activities which are not under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239(e)(4), you must determine whether the employee meets any of the time tests set forth in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239, paragraphs (e)(2) through (D). Under the facts you have stated, this employee might meet the requirements of paragraph (e)(2)(A), that is spending forty hours in two consecutive months influencing administrative activity, of which ten hours are spent in direct communication. In addition, you should be aware that the utility company, if it employs a lobbyist, must report the time spent by this employee in developing the material on which the lobbyist's communications are based, even though these activities are not includable by the employee in determining whether he is a lobbyist. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18620. The regulation does not apply if the employee spends less than ten percent of his time in developing such material. Copies of 2 Cal. Adm. Code 18239 and 18620 are enclosed. I hope this information will be of assistance. Sincerely, Natalie E. West Staff Counsel
